evidence: QueryExecutor interface maintains consistent parameter ordering (lemma, pattern/deprel, minLogDice, maxResults) across findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, and executeDependencyPatternWithPos

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern uses ExploreOptions to bundle 4 parameters, but exploreMultiSeed takes minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared as separate args — the same 3 parameters are bundled in ExploreOptions for single-seed but loose for multi-seed, creating an inconsistent calling convention between the two exploration paths

HTTP API mixes `relation` as a URL path segment (`/api/sketch/{lemma}/{relation}`) with `relation` as a query parameter (`/api/semantic-field/explore?relation=adj_predicate`) — both usages exist without documentation of why one pattern was chosen per endpoint
handleConcordanceExamples uses `word1` and `word2` as parameter names while handleSemanticFieldExamples uses `adjective` and `noun` — both endpoints take a word pair but parameterise it differently

The public SemanticFieldExplorer API has two overloads of exploreByPattern (a convenience form taking RelationConfig and a raw form taking individual strings) — the raw form is public but should be private since it exposes internal details (simplePattern) to callers
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates places the optional `posMap` parameter last — consistent with the recommendation to put optional parameters last in the method signature

strengths: QueryExecutor interface parameter ordering is uniform across all 6 query methods — a developer learning one method can predict the signature of the others
Optional `posMap` parameter is consistently placed last in CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates with a @Nullable annotation
The ExploreOptions record successfully bundles 4 parameters for the single-seed path, reducing argument lists from 7+ to 3
HTTP REST endpoints follow REST resource naming conventions (nouns, not verbs) for most routes
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The coherence issues are surface-area inconsistencies — the same parameters are bundled in some methods and scattered in others, and sibling endpoints use different parameter naming conventions for conceptually identical inputs.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The QueryExecutor interface is a well-designed consistent API. The cracks appear at the boundary between the exploration layer (single-seed vs multi-seed parameter conventions) and between HTTP endpoints (word1/word2 vs adjective/noun). These inconsistencies make the API somewhat unpredictable for a developer who learns one endpoint and expects the pattern to generalise. The score is above 70 because the core query interface is consistent and the HTTP surface is small; it falls below 80 because the parameter inconsistencies are in the most externally visible layer.

issues.summary: exploreMultiSeed takes 3 numeric parameters (minLogDice, topCollocates, minShared) individually while exploreByPattern bundles identical parameters into ExploreOptions
evidence: `exploreByPattern(String seed, RelationConfig, ExploreOptions opts)` — 3 parameters, options bundled
`exploreMultiSeed(Set<String> seeds, RelationConfig, double minLogDice, int topCollocates, int minShared)` — 5 parameters, same 3 numeric values as loose args
ExplorationHandlers calls both methods; the caller must unpack ExploreParams fields and re-pass them individually for the multi-seed path
issues.suggestion: Add an ExploreOptions overload for exploreMultiSeed, or document in ExploreOptions that its nounsPerSeed field is ignored for multi-seed and use the same object for both paths; this aligns the calling convention and reduces the caller's bookkeeping

issues.summary: handleConcordanceExamples uses word1/word2 while handleSemanticFieldExamples uses adjective/noun for the same semantic concept (a word pair)
evidence: Both endpoints take a word pair and return example sentences; the different parameter names are not explained by documentation
issues.suggestion: Standardise to the more descriptive names (adjective/noun) in handleConcordanceExamples since the domain meaning is the same; or document why word1/word2 is intentionally more generic for the concordance endpoint

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern raw overload (taking simplePattern string) is public but exposes an internal implementation detail
evidence: `public ExplorationResult exploreByPattern(String seed, String relationName, String bcqlPattern, String simplePattern, ExploreOptions opts)` — callers must supply both the BCQL pattern and a simplePattern fallback
The Javadoc on the convenience RelationConfig overload says it 'mirrors the delegation style used by the multi-seed path'
No external caller of the raw overload exists in the codebase — only the convenience overload is called, making the public raw overload an unnecessarily large API surface
issues.suggestion: Reduce the raw overload to package-private or protected visibility; external callers should use the RelationConfig-taking convenience overload which hides the simplePattern detail

root_causes: The ExploreOptions record was added for single-seed exploration and not retrofitted to multi-seed when MultiSeedExplorer was extracted
likely_symptoms: word-pair-param-naming will surface as a client integration friction point when developers switch between endpoints
